sections.title: Definitions of key terms in Cybercrimes Act
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Cybercrimes Act, including 'data', 'computer', 'computer system', and various legal references. It specifies that 'data' refers to electronic representations of information in any form, while 'computer system' encompasses individual computers or interconnected systems. The definitions also include references to other relevant legislation such as the Constitution and the Protection of Personal Information Act.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish foundational terms for data processing and system interactions, which are crucial for understanding data transfers and system security requirements in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: ‘‘data’’ means electronic representations of information in any form
‘‘computer’’ means any electronic programmable device used to perform predetermined arithmetic, logical, routing, processing or storage operations
‘‘computer system’’ means one computer or two or more interconnected computers

sections.heading: (1) Any person who unlawfully and intentionally intercepts data, including
sections.title: Unlawful interception of data in South African context
sections.summary: This section defines offences for unlawfully intercepting data, including electromagnetic emissions from computer systems, and possessing intercepted data with knowledge of its unlawful origin. It also establishes that possession of data with reasonable suspicion of unlawful interception without a satisfactory explanation constitutes an offence. The term 'interception of data' is explicitly defined to include acquisition, viewing, capturing, copying, and diversion of data.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses data interception, which could impact cross-border data transfers if interception occurs during international data flows. It establishes legal consequences for intercepting data, relevant to data security and integrity in international contexts.

requirements.text: Any person who unlawfully and intentionally intercepts data, including electromagnetic emissions from a computer system carrying such data, within or which is transmitted to or from a computer system, is guilty of an offence.

Any person who unlawfully and intentionally possesses data or the output of data, with the knowledge that such data was intercepted unlawfully as contemplated in subsection (1), is guilty of an offence.

Any person who is found in possession of data or the output of data, in regard to which there is a reasonable suspicion that such data was intercepted unlawfully as contemplated in subsection (1) and who is unable to give a satisfactory exculpatory account of such possession, is guilty of an offence.

Interception of data means the acquisition, viewing, capturing or copying of data of a non-public nature through the use of a hardware or software tool contemplated in section 4(2) or any other means, so as to make some or all of the data available to a person, other than the lawful owner or holder of the data, the sender or the recipient or the intended recipient of that data.
Interception of data includes examination or inspection of the contents of the data.
Interception of data includes diversion of the data or any part thereof from its intended destination to any other destination.

sections.title: Unlawful use and possession of software or hardware tools
sections.summary: This section prohibits the unlawful use or possession of software or hardware tools intended for intercepting data, accessing systems, or committing other offences under the Cybercrimes Act. It defines 'software or hardware tool' as any electronic, mechanical, or other instrument designed primarily for accessing systems, intercepting data, or interfering with data and computer systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses the use of tools for data interception, which could impact data security during cross-border transfers. It establishes legal consequences for using tools that facilitate unlawful data access or interception.

requirements.text: Any person who unlawfully and intentionally uses or possesses any software or hardware tool with the purpose of contravening the provisions of article 2(1) or (2), 3(1), 5(1), 6(1) or 7(1)(a) or (d), is guilty of an offence.

Software or hardware tool means any electronic, mechanical or other instrument, device, equipment, apparatus or a substantial component thereof or a computer program, which is designed or adapted primarily for the purpose to access as contemplated in article 2(1) or (2), intercept data as contemplated in article 3(1), interfere with data or a computer program as contemplated in article 5(1), interfere with a computer data storage medium or a computer system as contemplated in article 6(1), or acquire, make available or use a password, access code or similar data or device as defined in article 7(3).

sections.heading: In Part II, unless the context indicates otherwise—
sections.title: Definitions for cybercrime data messages
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in cybercrime offenses, including 'damage to property', 'disclose', 'group of persons', 'related person', and 'violence'. These definitions specify what constitutes electronic communication threats and the scope of protected individuals. The definitions apply to persons committing cybercrimes under the Act.

requirements.text: ‘‘damage to property’’ means damage to any corporeal or incorporeal property
‘‘disclose’’ in respect of a data message referred to in sections 14, 15 and 16 means to send the data message to an intended recipient or any other person
‘‘disclose’’ in respect of a data message referred to in sections 14, 15 and 16 means to store the data message on an electronic communications network where it can be viewed, copied or downloaded

‘‘disclose’’ in respect of a data message referred to in sections 14, 15 and 16 means to send or make available a link to the data message stored on an electronic communications network where it can be viewed, copied or downloaded

‘‘group of persons’’ means characteristics that identify an individual as a member of a group, including race, gender, sex, pregnancy, marital status, ethnic or social origin, colour, sexual orientation, age, disability, religion, conscience, belief, culture, language, birth or nationality
‘‘related person’’ means any member of the family or household of a person or any other person in a close relationship with that person

sections.title: Penalties for contravening cybercrime provisions
sections.summary: This section specifies penalties for individuals who violate certain cybercrime provisions. Contravention of specific sections (2(1)/(2), 3(3), 7(2)) incurs a fine or imprisonment up to five years. Violation of sections (3(1)/(2), 4(1), 5(1), 6(1), 7(1)) carries penalties up to ten years. Violation of section 11(1) results in penalties up to fifteen years. Courts may impose sentences based on Criminal Procedure Act, 1977, for offenses not otherwise prescribed. Courts must consider electronic means as aggravating factors for theft offenses.

sections.title: Protection orders for data messages
sections.summary: This section establishes procedures for obtaining protection orders to prevent disclosure of data messages in cybercrime cases. A claimant can apply for a protection order to prevent disclosure of data messages by individuals or electronic communications service providers. The court may issue orders to identify the person who disclosed the data message or the service provider. The order may be amended or set aside within 14 days. Failure to comply with the order constitutes an offense.

sections.title: Jurisdiction for cybercrime offences in specific scenarios
sections.summary: This section defines when a court in the Republic has jurisdiction to try cybercrime offences. It specifies conditions including the accused's nationality or residency, the offence's location, the offence's connection to Republic-registered assets, and the offence's impact on Republic entities. The jurisdiction applies to offences committed within the Republic or on Republic-registered vessels, ships, offshore installations, platforms, or aircraft.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes jurisdictional conditions for cybercrime cases involving Republic-registered assets or locations, which may impact data transfer assessments when cross-border data processing involves Republic-registered systems or entities.

The offence was committed in the territory of the Republic or on board a vessel, ship, offshore installation, fixed platform, or aircraft registered in the Republic

sections.title: Search warrant execution powers for law enforcement
sections.summary: This section details the specific powers a police officer may exercise under a search warrant. It specifies that the warrant must identify the person to be searched, the premises or items to be searched, and the scope of the search. sections.title: Oral applications for search warrants
sections.summary: This section allows for oral applications for search warrants in cases where it is not reasonably practicable to make a written application. The oral application must indicate the urgency of the case or exceptional circumstances justifying the oral application and comply with any supplementary directives from the Chief Justice. A warrant may be issued by a magistrate or judge of the High Court upon an oral application, provided that the magistrate or judge is satisfied on the facts that there are reasonable grounds to believe a warrant could be issued, it is necessary immediately, and it is not reasonably practicable to make a written application.

sections.title: Prohibition on disclosure of information obtained in official duties
sections.summary: No person, investigator, police official, electronic communications service provider, financial institution, or employee of such entities may disclose information obtained in the exercise of their powers or duties under Chapter 4 or 5 of the Act, except for specific exceptions including sharing with other entities for cybercrime prevention, court-ordered evidence, or foreign authorities in cybercrime investigations. Unauthorized disclosure is punishable by a fine or imprisonment up to three years.

requirements.text: No person, investigator, police official, electronic communications service provider, financial institution, or employee of such entities may disclose information obtained in the exercise of their powers or duties under Chapter 4 or 5 of the Act

Disclosure may be made if it is required in terms of any law or as evidence in any court of law
Disclosure may be made for information-sharing between entities aimed at preventing, detecting, investigating or mitigating cybercrime, provided it does not prejudice criminal investigations
Disclosure may be made to foreign authorities if they require it for cybercrime prevention, detection, or mitigation
The prohibition does not apply if disclosure is authorized by the Act or another law, or reveals criminal activity

sections.title: Admissibility of affidavits and declarations in criminal proceedings
sections.summary: This section establishes conditions under which affidavits or solemn/attested declarations are admissible as prima facie evidence in criminal or civil proceedings. It specifies that such documents must be signed by individuals claiming to be in the service of a Republic or foreign state designated by the Cabinet member responsible for justice, and must be authenticated through court procedures or the Justices of the Peace and Commissioners of Oaths Act. The section also outlines procedures for courts to subpoena witnesses or submit written interrogatories when affidavits are presented.

sections.title: Reporting obligations for electronic service providers and financial institutions
sections.summary: This section requires electronic communications service providers and financial institutions to report certain cybercrimes to the South African Police Service within 72 hours of becoming aware of an offence. sections.title: Cross-border transfer of financial data involving R50,000+ amounts
sections.summary: This section defines offences under the Cybercrimes Act that involve cross-border transfers of financial data where the amount is R50,000 or more. It specifies that offences related to the Cybercrimes Act (sections 3(1), 5, 6, 7(1), 8, 9(1), (2), or 10) and substantially similar offences committed in foreign states involving R50,000 or more are subject to this provision. The offences apply to individuals or entities committing financial misconduct across jurisdictions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a threshold for cross-border financial data transfers involving R50,000 or more, requiring specific safeguards for international transfers of financial data that could impact data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: Any offence referred to in section 3(1), 5, 6, 7(1), 8, 9(1) or (2), or 10 of the Cybercrimes Act involving R50,000 or more
Any offence substantially similar to those in the above list committed in a foreign state involving R50,000 or more

sections.source_quote: Any offence referred to in— (a) section 3(1), 5, 6, 7(1), 8, 9(1) or (2), or 10; or (b) section 17 (in so far as the section relates to the offences referred to in paragraph (a)), of the Cybercrimes Act, 2020, which involves an amount of R50 000, 00 or more.

sections.title: Harmful disclosure of pornography and protection orders
sections.summary: This section outlines offences related to harmful disclosure of pornography where the disclosure occurs without consent and causes harm to the person depicted or their family. It also establishes procedures for courts to issue protection orders against such disclosures, including orders to remove pornography from electronic communications services. The offences apply to individuals who disclose or threaten to disclose pornography without consent, causing harm to the victim.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses cross-border data transfers related to harmful pornography disclosures, requiring specific legal protections for victims and electronic service providers.

requirements.text: A person who unlawfully and intentionally discloses or causes disclosure of pornography in which a person appears or is described without consent and causes harm to that person or their family
A person who unlawfully and intentionally threatens to disclose or cause disclosure of pornography without consent, causing harm to the person or their family
A person who unlawfully and intentionally threatens to disclose or cause disclosure of pornography for extortion purposes
A complainant may apply to a magistrate's court for a protection order to prohibit disclosure or access to pornography
An electronic communications service provider must remove or disable access to pornography if ordered by a court

sections.source_quote: A person (‘A') who unlawfully and intentionally discloses or causes the disclosure of pornography in which a person (‘B') appears or is described and such disclosure— (a) takes place without the consent of B; and (b) causes any harm, including mental, psychological, physical, social or economic harm, to B or any member of the family of B or any other person in a close relationship to B, is guilty of the offence of harmful disclosure of pornography.

sections.title: Subpoena enforcement for electronic communications service providers
sections.summary: This section outlines the obligations for electronic communications service providers and individuals subpoenaed to attend court proceedings. It specifies that a subpoenaed person must appear within 14 days to provide evidence, documents, or objects if the court deems it essential for the case. Failure to comply with these requirements constitutes an offense under this section.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for electronic communications service providers to comply with court subpoenas, which may impact data transfer when evidence is required across jurisdictions. It specifies timelines and consequences for non-compliance.
